articles.title: Claude Science is Anthropic’s newest flagship product
articles.subtitle: At an event for pharmaceutical executives, biotech founders, and researchers on Tuesday, Anthropic announced Claude Science, a major new product intended to support scientific research in the same way that Claude Code supports software engi
articles.author: Grace Huckins
articles.summary: At an event for pharmaceutical executives, biotech founders, and researchers on Tuesday, Anthropic announced Claude Science, a major new product intended to support scientific research in the same way that Claude Code supports software engineering. Like Claude Code, Claude Science can autonomously carry out meaningful work when given concise, high-level instructions, and it has access…
body: At an event for pharmaceutical executives, biotech founders, and researchers on Tuesday, Anthropic announced Claude Science, a major new product intended to support scientific research in the same way that Claude Code supports software engineering. Like Claude Code, Claude Science can autonomously carry out meaningful work when given concise, high-level instructions, and it has access to tools that make it particularly useful for research in computational biology and drug development. Along with launching and previewing Claude Science, which is now available to all paid Claude subscribers, Anthropic also announced that it will be using the product to pursue some of its own research into drugs for rare, neglected diseases.

This is not Anthropic’s first foray into AI for science. In October, the company released plug-ins that help Claude make use of scientific software and databases under the heading “Claude for Life Sciences.” But unlike this earlier release, Claude Science is a full-featured, standalone product. Anthropic’s decision to elevate Claude Science to the same rank as Claude Code and Claude Cowork indicates that the company is taking AI’s scientific applications very seriously—or at least wants to give the impression that it is.

“It represents how important this is to our mission that this is right up there with Claude Code and Claude Cowork as the next really significant product that we’re releasing,” says Eric Kauderer-Abrams, Anthropic’s head of life sciences. “Our mission is to develop AI that serves humanity’s long-term well-being, and we believe that by far the greatest opportunity to do that is in the life sciences.”

For the past decade, one company—Google DeepMind—has been at the vanguard of AI for science. CEO Demis Hassabis and researcher John Jumper won the Nobel Prize in chemistry for their work on the company’s AlphaFold model, and DeepMind has also made major contributions to meteorology, materials science, and a variety of other disciplines. But in the past several months, the fast-advancing frontier of AI progress seems to have left DeepMind in the dust. When it comes to coding, which has become the most lucrative use case for LLMs, DeepMind is stuck playing catch-up .

Anthropic is well positioned to take up DeepMind’s scientific mantle. Like Hassabis, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei is a PhD scientist—unlike OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, who’s a businessman through and through. Many scientists are already avid users of tools such as Claude Code. These days, a lot of scientific research involves some amount of coding, but not all scientists are expert software engineers, and so tools like Claude Code can make a huge difference for their productivity. And the company has recently earned a major scientific vote of confidence: Earlier this month, Jumper announced that he is leaving DeepMind for Anthropic.

Since agents powered by LLMs, including Anthropic’s Opus model series, became capable of useful, independent work in late 2025, scientists have been seeing just how much they can do. In a blog post published on Anthropic’s website, the Harvard physicist Matthew Schwartz estimated, on the basis of his work with Claude Code and other Anthropic tools, that the company’s Opus 4.5 model is about as capable of executing scientific projects as a second-year graduate student.

According to Kauderer-Abrams, Claude Science isn’t intended to displace Claude Code and Claude Cowork in scientists’ workflows. Instead, it’s designed to build on what scientists already find useful about Anthropic’s products. For instance, it not only writes code but also helps scientists run their code on powerful computer clusters, which many many scientists need for their work but can be difficult to manage. And it prioritizes reproducibility, so that scientists can trace back the source of any figure or result and check it for accuracy and validity.

Though Claude Science could in principle assist with any area of scientific research, it seems designed and marketed as a tool for molecular and cellular biology, and for drug development in particular. It can interface with various tools used in genetics, chemistry, and protein biology, all of which could come in handy for researchers on the hunt for new drugs. During the Tuesday event, Alexander Tarashansky, who led the development of Claude Science, demonstrated how the system could autonomously identify new drug candidates for phenylketonuria, a rare genetic disease.

And Anthropic isn’t leaving all that work to the pharma companies and university labs that were represented at the event. Armed with Claude Science, it will be pursuing its own research into drug candidates for neglected diseases—both to help move science forward and to gain a clearer sense of how Claude Science works in the real world.

There are obvious humanitarian reasons to prioritize drug development when creating a general-purpose scientific research tool, and AI industry leaders often cite curing disease as a major potential upside of the technology. But it’s also notable that pharmaceutical companies have far deeper pockets than academic researchers. Anthropic says it’s set to see its first profitable quarter, and if major new contracts with pharmaceutical companies are forthcoming, they could help ensure it stays profitable as the tokenmaxxing craze dies down—something that’s ever more important as an IPO approaches later this year.
Launching next week on T-Mobile's network, the cell plan takes a nuclear approach to online safety.
Subquadratic has now shared more details about its new model. But some are still skeptical.
keyPoints: At an event for pharmaceutical executives, biotech founders, and researchers on Tuesday, Anthropic announced Claude Science, a major new product intended to support scientific research in the same way that Claude Code supports software engineering. Like Claude Code, Claude Science can autonomously carry out meaningful work when given concise, high-level instructions, and it has access to tools that make it particularly useful for research in computational biology and drug development. Along with launching and previewing Claude Science, which is now available to all paid Claude subscribers, Anthropic also announced that it will be using the product to pursue some of its own research into drugs for rare, neglected diseases.

This is not Anthropic’s first foray into AI for science. In October, the company released plug-ins that help Claude make use of scientific software and databases under the heading “Claude for Life Sciences.” But unlike this earlier release, Claude Science is a full-featured, standalone product. Anthropic’s decision to elevate Claude Science to the same rank as Claude Code and Claude Cowork indicates that the company is taking AI’s scientific applications very seriously—or at least wants to give the impression that it is.

“It represents how important this is to our mission that this is right up there with Claude Code and Claude Cowork as the next really significant product that we’re releasing,” says Eric Kauderer-Abrams, Anthropic’s head of life sciences. “Our mission is to develop AI that serves humanity’s long-term well-being, and we believe that by far the greatest opportunity to do that is in the life sciences.”
paragraphTranslations: 在周二的一个针对制药高管、生物技术创始人和研究人员的活动上，Anthropic宣布了Claude Science，这是一个旨在以Claude Code支持软件工程的方式支持科学研究的重大新产品。像Claude Code一样，Claude Science可以在给出简洁、高级的指令时自动执行有意义的工作，并且它可以使用工具，使其特别适合于计算生物学和药物开发的研究。除了推出和预览现在所有付费Claude订阅者都可以使用的Claude Science，Anthropic还宣布将使用该产品进行自己的研究，研究针对罕见、被忽视疾病药物。

这不是Anthropic首次涉足科学领域的人工智能。去年10月，该公司发布了插件，帮助Claude在“Claude for Life Sciences”的标题下使用科学软件和数据库。但与早期版本不同，Claude Science是一个功能齐全的独立产品。Anthropic将Claude Science提升到与Claude Code和Claude Cowork同等的地位，表明该公司非常重视人工智能的科学应用——或者至少希望给人留下这样的印象。
Anthropic的生命科学负责人Eric Kauderer-Abrams说：“这代表了这对我们的使命有多重要，这与Claude Code和Claude Cowork一样，是我们即将发布的下一个真正重要的产品。我们的使命是开发为人类长期福祉服务的人工智能，我们相信，迄今为止，最大的机会在于生命科学。”

在过去的十年里，一家公司——谷歌DeepMind——一直处于科学领域人工智能的前沿。首席执行官Demis Hassabis和研究员John Jumper因为他们在公司的AlphaFold模型上的的工作赢得了诺贝尔化学奖，DeepMind还对气象学、材料科学和其他许多学科做出了重大贡献。但在过去几个月里，人工智能进步的快速发展似乎让DeepMind落后了。在编码方面，这已经成为LLMs最有利可图的用例，DeepMind还在努力迎头赶上。

Anthropic完全有能力接过DeepMind的科学衣钵。像Hassabis一样，Anthropic的首席执行官Dario Amodei是一位拥有博士学位的科学家——与彻头彻尾的商人、OpenAI的首席执行官Sam Altman不同。许多科学家已经是Claude Code等工具的狂热用户。如今，许多科学研究涉及一定程度的编码，但并非所有科学家都是软件工程师专家，因此像Claude Code这样的工具可以极大地提高他们的生产力。该公司最近还获得了重大的科学信任票：本月早些时候，Jumper宣布他将离开DeepMind加入Anthropic。
自从2025年底由LLMs驱动的代理（包括Anthropic的Opus模型系列）能够进行有用的独立工作以来，科学家们一直在观察它们能做多少。在Anthropic网站上发表的一篇博客文章中，哈佛大学物理学家Matthew Schwartz根据他与Claude Code和其他Anthropic工具的工作估计，该公司的Opus 4.5模型能够执行科学项目的能力强于二年级研究生。

Kauderer-Abrams表示，Claude Science并不打算取代科学家工作流程中的Claude Code和Claude Cowork。相反，它的设计是为了建立在科学家已经发现Anthropic产品有用的基础上。例如，它不仅编写代码，还帮助科学家在强大的计算机集群上运行他们的代码，许多科学家需要这些集群来完成工作，但管理起来可能很困难。它还优先考虑可复制性，以便科学家可以追溯任何图表或结果的来源，并检查其准确性和有效性。

虽然Claude Science理论上可以协助任何领域的科学研究，但它似乎被设计和市场定位为分子和细胞生物学的工具，特别是药物开发。它可以与遗传学、化学和蛋白质生物学中使用的各种工具进行接口，所有这些对寻找新药的研究人员来说都可能很有用。在周二的活动上，领导Claude Science开发的Alexander Tarashansky展示了该系统如何能够自动识别苯丙酮尿症这一罕见遗传病的新药候选物。
Anthropic并没有将所有这些工作留给参加该活动的制药公司和大学实验室。凭借Claude Science，它将进行自己的研究，研究针对被忽视疾病药物候选物——既帮助推进科学发展，也更清楚地了解Claude Science在现实世界中的工作方式。

在创建通用科学研究工具时，优先考虑药物开发有明显的人道主义原因，人工智能行业领导者经常引用治愈疾病作为技术的主要潜在好处。但值得注意的是，制药公司的财力远远超过学术研究人员。Anthropic表示，它将看到第一个盈利季度，如果与制药公司签订的主要新合同即将到来，它们可能有助于确保随着tokenmaxxing热潮消退，Anthropic保持盈利——随着今年晚些时候首次公开募股（IPO）的临近，这一点变得越来越重要。
下周在T-Mobile的网络上推出的手机计划对网络安全采取了核方法。
Subquadratic现在已经分享了更多关于其新模型的细节。但仍有人持怀疑态度。

Claude Science, available to paid Claude subscribers, operates autonomously with high-level instructions and has tools that enhance its research capabilities. Anthropic also plans to use Claude Science for its own drug research into rare diseases. This move positions Anthropic to challenge Google DeepMind's leadership in AI for science, as Anthropic's CEO, Dario Amodei, unlike OpenAI's Sam Altman, has a scientific background, and many scientists already use tools like Claude Code.

articles.title: Building tech in the world’s secret R&D hub
articles.subtitle: Apple. Anthropic. Disney Research. Google. Meta. Microsoft. NVIDIA. OpenAI. Few places outside Silicon Valley can claim R&D hubs from all of these companies. Fewer still are concentrated in a city of just over 400,000 people—roughly half th
articles.author: Greater Zurich Area
articles.summary: Apple. Anthropic. Disney Research. Google. Meta. Microsoft. NVIDIA. OpenAI. Few places outside Silicon Valley can claim R&D hubs from all of these companies. Fewer still are concentrated in a city of just over 400,000 people—roughly half the size of San Francisco. Over the past two decades, however, many of the world’s most influential technology companies…
body: Zurich has created a technology ecosystem nearing the density of Silicon Valley.

Apple. Anthropic. Disney Research. Google. Meta. Microsoft. NVIDIA. OpenAI. Few places outside Silicon Valley can claim R&D hubs from all of these companies. Fewer still are concentrated in a city of just over 400,000 people—roughly half the size of San Francisco.

Over the past two decades, however, many of the world's most influential technology companies have established R&D operations in and around Zurich, Switzerland. What began with Google's decision to build its largest R&D hub outside the United States has evolved into one of the world’s most concentrated centers for AI research, talent, and commercialization, in certain areas at a higher density than Silicon Valley.
The question is why so many technology leaders keep choosing the same place to build and scale.

Located at the center of Europe, Greater Zurich Area, a region spanning the cantons of Glarus, Graubünden, Schaffhausen, Schwyz, Solothurn, Tessin, Uri, Zug, and Zürich, the region of Winterthur, and the city of Zurich, combines access to major markets with political stability, regulatory predictability, and strong intellectual property protection. And Zurich Airport connects the region directly with key business hubs across Europe, North America, and Asia, making it an efficient base for international operations.

The country's innovation performance reinforces this position. Switzerland has ranked first in the Global Innovation Index for more than a decade, leads the world in patents per capita, and invests over 3.3% of GDP in research and development. Earlier this year, google.org pledged a $1 million grant to the Swiss National AI Institute, a joint effort to advance AI research for the public good.

Switzerland’s venture ecosystem reflects a similar focus. Over 60% of Swiss venture capital is invested in deep tech—the highest share globally by a large margin and nearly twice the share of major economies like Germany, France, and the UK. And, according to the Swiss Deep Tech Report 2026 , at $1,470 invested per capita, Switzerland commits more to deep tech per capita than any other country in Europe.

While Switzerland is one of Europe's most expensive locations for talent and operations, salaries remain at a fraction of those in Silicon Valley. The talent pool is small by global standards. Scaling a team quickly is harder in Zurich than in London, Paris, or Amsterdam. For early-stage companies that need to hire fast and burn lean, that trade-off is real. For companies building specialized AI capabilities, however, the equation works: The objective is to assemble the right team, not the largest one.

Switzerland's economy is built around high-value, knowledge-intensive work. Productivity is among the highest in the world, and companies concentrate on functions that depend on specialized expertise rather than large workforces. For companies developing advanced AI capabilities, cost is often weighed against factors that are harder to replicate elsewhere: direct access to leading universities and research institutions, regulatory stability, and a quality of life that helps attract and retain skilled international talent.
Within Switzerland, the Greater Zurich Area concentrates many of the ingredients required to build and deploy AI systems.

The defining characteristic of this region is density. Many of the world's leading AI companies, research institutions, investors, and startups operate in close proximity, creating connections between talent, capital, and ideas.

For example, Google engineers teach at ETH Zurich. ETH graduates join companies such as Anthropic. Researchers launch startups, while former employees of global technology firms go on to found new ventures of their own. Investors, founders, academics, and corporate teams encounter each other repeatedly through shared networks, industry events, and professional circles. In a region of this size, collaboration often happens less through formal introductions than through proximity. While talent flows freely, it rarely leaves the ecosystem.
keyPoints: Zurich has created a technology ecosystem nearing the density of Silicon Valley.

Apple. Anthropic. Disney Research. Google. Meta. Microsoft. NVIDIA. OpenAI. Few places outside Silicon Valley can claim R&D hubs from all of these companies. Fewer still are concentrated in a city of just over 400,000 people—roughly half the size of San Francisco.

Over the past two decades, however, many of the world's most influential technology companies have established R&D operations in and around Zurich, Switzerland. What began with Google's decision to build its largest R&D hub outside the United States has evolved into one of the world’s most concentrated centers for AI research, talent, and commercialization, in certain areas at a higher density than Silicon Valley.
paragraphTranslations: 苏黎世创造了一个接近硅谷密度的技术生态系统。
苹果。人类公司。迪士尼研究。谷歌。Meta。微软。英伟达。OpenAI。在硅谷之外，很少有地方能声称拥有所有这些公司的R&D中心。更少的地方集中在一个仅有40多万人口的城市——大约是旧金山的一半大小。
然而在过去二十年里，许多世界上最有影响力的技术公司在瑞士苏黎世及其周边地区建立了研发业务。以谷歌决定在美国以外建立其最大的研发中心为起点，已经发展成为世界上人工智能研究、人才和商业化的最集中中心之一，在某些领域的密度甚至高于硅谷。
问题是为什么这么多技术领袖不断选择同一个地方来建设和扩展。
位于欧洲中心的大苏黎世地区，跨越格拉鲁斯、格劳宾登、沙夫豪森、施维茨、索洛图恩、特辛、乌里、楚格和苏黎世州，以及温特图尔地区和苏黎世市，将进入主要市场的机会与政治稳定、监管可预测性和强大的知识产权保护结合起来。苏黎世机场将该地区直接与欧洲、北美和亚洲的关键商业中心连接起来，使其成为国际业务的高效基地。
该国的创新表现加强了这一地位。瑞士在全球创新指数中排名第一已有十多年，人均专利领先世界，并在研发上投资超过GDP的3.3%。今年早些时候，google.org向瑞士国家人工智能研究所承诺了100万美元的赠款，这是一项共同推进人工智能研究以造福公众的努力。
瑞士的风险生态系统反映了类似的重点。超过60%的瑞士风险投资投资于深科技——这一比例在全球范围内是最高的，几乎是德国、法国和英国等主要经济体的两倍。根据2026年瑞士深科技报告，瑞士人均投资于深科技的资金为1470美元，比欧洲任何其他国家都多。
虽然瑞士是欧洲人才和运营成本最高的地区之一，但工资仍然是硅谷的一小部分。按照国际标准，人才库很小。在苏黎世快速扩展团队比在伦敦、巴黎或阿姆斯特丹更难。对于需要快速招聘和精简运营的初创公司来说，这种权衡是真实的。然而，对于构建专业人工智能能力的公司来说，这个等式是可行的：目标是组建正确的团队，而不是最大的团队。
瑞士的经济是建立在高价值、知识密集型工作的基础上的。生产力在世界上是最高的，公司专注于依赖专业知识而不是大量劳动力的功能。对于开发先进人工智能能力的公司来说，成本往往是相对于其他地方更难复制的因素：直接接触领先的大学和研究机构、监管稳定以及有助于吸引和留住熟练国际人才的生活质量。
在瑞士，大苏黎世地区集中了许多构建和部署人工智能系统所需的要素。
这个地区的定义特征是密度。许多世界领先的人工智能公司、研究机构、投资者和初创公司在紧密的邻近地区运营，创建了人才、资本和创意之间的联系。
例如，谷歌工程师在苏黎世联邦理工学院教学。苏黎世联邦理工学院的毕业生加入了人类公司等公司。研究人员创办初创公司，而全球科技公司的前员工继续创办自己的新企业。投资者、创始人、学者和企业团队通过共享网络、行业活动和专业圈子反复相遇。在这个大小的地区，合作往往不是通过正式介绍，而是通过邻近发生。虽然人才自由流动，但很少离开生态系统。

vocabulary.example: The technology ecosystem in Zurich is thriving.
vocabulary.exampleZh: 苏黎世的技术生态系统正在蓬勃发展。

vocabulary.example: Many companies have their R&D hubs in Zurich.
vocabulary.exampleZh: 许多公司在苏黎世设有研发中心。

vocabulary.example: Switzerland leads the world in patents per capita.
vocabulary.exampleZh: 瑞士在人均专利方面领先世界。

vocabulary.example: Investment in R&D is crucial for innovation.
vocabulary.exampleZh: 在研发方面的投资对创新至关重要。

vocabulary.example: The proximity of AI companies in Zurich fosters collaboration.
vocabulary.exampleZh: 苏黎世人工智能公司的邻近促进了合作。

vocabulary.example: Attracting and retaining talent is key for tech companies.
vocabulary.exampleZh: 吸引和留住人才对科技公司至关重要。

paragraphs: Zurich has become a global center for technology research and development, rivaling Silicon Valley in density. Major companies like Apple, Google, and Meta have established R&D hubs in Zurich, Switzerland, attracted by its central European location, political stability, and strong intellectual property protection. Zurich Airport provides direct connections to key business hubs worldwide, and Switzerland's innovation performance, leading in patents per capita and R&D investment, reinforces its position. Despite high costs, salaries in Zurich are lower than in Silicon Valley, and the focus is on specialized expertise rather than large workforces. The Greater Zurich Area is a dense ecosystem where AI companies, research institutions, and startups operate in close proximity, fostering collaboration and talent retention.
paragraphTranslations: 苏黎世已经成为全球技术研究和开发中心，其密集程度可与硅谷媲美。苹果、谷歌和Meta等大公司在瑞士苏黎世建立了研发中心，它们被其位于欧洲中心的地理位置、政治稳定性和强有力的知识产权保护所吸引。苏黎世机场提供了与全球主要商业中心的直接联系，而瑞士在人均专利和研发投资方面的创新表现也加强了其地位。尽管成本高昂，苏黎世的薪资低于硅谷，且重点在于专业技能而非大量劳动力。大苏黎世地区是一个密集的生态系统，人工智能公司、研究机构和初创企业在近距离内运营，促进了合作和人才留存。
